questions.text: In the preparation of a soap, the fatty acid containing less than eight carbon atoms is not used, because the soap obtained:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Forms a colloidal solution with water
options.explanation: Soaps are not colloidal solutions, they are soluble in water. A colloidal solution is a type of mixture in which the dispersed particles are much smaller than 1 micrometer. Soap molecules are much larger than this, so they would not form a colloidal solution with water.

This is true, but it is not the reason why fatty acids with less than eight carbon atoms are not used in the preparation of soap. As I mentioned before, the main reason is that these acids have short hydrophobic tails that cannot form strong interactions with grease.

options.text: Does not form an emulsion with the grease
options.explanation: Soap has a tadpole-like structure because it possesses a long carboxylic acid chain through which hydrocarbons and sodium-potassium ions are attached. Ions dissolve in water and a non-ionic carbon chain dissolves in oil and forms an emulsion but to form an emulsion non-ionic chain must have more than 8 carbon atoms.

options.text: Forms an emulsion with the grease
options.explanation: This is true, but only if the fatty acids used to make the soap have long enough hydrophobic tails. Fatty acids with less than eight carbon atoms have short hydrophobic tails, so they cannot form strong interactions with grease.

questions.explanation: Soap has a tadpole like structure, because they possess a long carboxylic acid chain through which hydrocarbons and sodium-pottasium ions are attached. The ionic part dissolves in water whereas the non-ionic carbon chain dissolves in oil and forms an emulsion, but to form an emulsion, the non-ionic chain must have more than 8 carbon atoms.

questions.text: Which of the following processes is done to make the soap transparent?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Glycerol and gum are added to the soap.
options.explanation: Adding glycerol and gum to soap will not make it transparent. Glycerin is a clear liquid, but it is not a solvent for soap. Gum is a type of thickener, and it would only make the soap opaque.Hence, this is not the correct option.

options.text: The soap is dissolved in alcohol and then the alcohol is evaporated out.
options.explanation: To make the soap transparent firstly soap crystals are processed with solvents like glycerine, alcohol, and sugar to dissolve, allowing light to pass through it, then further the solvent is evaporated by heating the solution therefore soap becomes transparent.Hence, this is the correct answer.

options.text: Glycerine is added to the alcoholic solution of soap.
options.explanation: Adding glycerine to the alcoholic solution of soap will not make it transparent either. Glycerine is a clear liquid, but it is not a solvent for soap. It would only make the soap more viscous, but it would not make it transparent.Hence, this is not the correct option.

options.text: A saturated solution of sodium chloride is added to the soap.
options.explanation: Adding a saturated solution of sodium chloride to soap will not make it transparent. Sodium chloride is a white solid, and it would only make the soap opaque.

questions.explanation: To make the soap transparent firstly soap crystals are processed with solvents like glycerine, alcohol and sugar to dissolve, that allowing light to pass through it, then further the solvent is evaporated by heating the solution therefore soap becomes transparent.

questions.text: The saturated solution of sodium chloride is added in the hot process of preparation of soap, because:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: It increases the hardness of soap
options.explanation: This is not correct. The saturated solution of sodium chloride does not increase the hardness of the soap. In fact, it can actually make the soap softer. This is because the salting out process forces the soap molecules to pack together more loosely.

options.text: It increases the solubility of soap in water
options.explanation: This is also not correct. The saturated solution of sodium chloride decreases the solubility of soap in water. This is because the sodium chloride ions in the solution compete with the soap molecules for the water molecules. As a result, the soap molecules are forced to come together and form solid crystals.

options.text: It decreases the solubility of soap in water
options.explanation: The solubility of soap in water depends upon the non-ionic or carbohydrate part of soap, as the sodium chloride is added in the hot process of preparation of soap the ionic part of soap increases therefore solubility of soap in water decreases.

options.text: It decreases the surface tension of water
options.explanation: This is not correct. The saturated solution of sodium chloride does not affect the surface tension of water.

questions.explanation: The solubility of soap in water depends upon the non-ionic or carbohydrate part of soap, as the sodium chloride is added in the hot process of preparation of soap the ionic part of soap increases therefore solubility of soap in water decreases.

questions.text: What is the type of particle formed after the contact of sodium stearate molecules with grease particle?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Neutral is incorrect because the formation of micelles involves the aggregation of charged particles (carboxylate ions) around the grease particles, resulting in a net negative charge.

Negatively charged is correct because when sodium stearate molecules come into contact with grease particles, they form negatively charged particles called micelles. Sodium stearate is a soap molecule composed of a hydrophilic (water-attracting) head and a hydrophobic (water-repelling) tail. In the presence of water, the hydrophilic heads orient themselves towards the water while the hydrophobic tails cluster together, enclosing the grease particles within the micelle. Since the hydrophilic heads contain negatively charged carboxylate ions, the micelles formed are negatively charged.
Positively charged is incorrect because sodium stearate molecules contain negatively charged carboxylate ions in their hydrophilic heads, leading to the formation of negatively charged micelles.

options.text: Positively charged from inside and negatively charged from outside
options.explanation: Positively charged from inside and negatively charged from outside is incorrect because micelles formed from sodium stearate molecules are uniformly negatively charged throughout their structure due to the presence of carboxylate ions in the hydrophilic heads. There is no internal positive charge.

questions.explanation: When sodium stearate comes in contact with grease particles, micelles are formed. These are negatively charged particles with hydrophillic heads and hydrophobic tails.

questions.text: Which of the following represents liquid propellants?

questions.text: Which one of the following regions of atmosphere contains ozone?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The troposphere is the lowest layer of Earth's atmosphere. Most of the mass (about 75-80%) of the atmosphere is in the troposphere. Most types of clouds are found in the troposphere, and almost all weather occurs within this layer.

The thermosphere is the atmospheric region from ∼85 to ∼500 km altitude, containing the ionosphere. It is characterized by high temperature and large variability, in response to changes in solar ultraviolet radiation and solar-driven geomagnetic activity.

The mesosphere lies between the thermosphere and the stratosphere. “Meso” means middle, and this is the highest layer of the atmosphere in which the gases are all mixed up rather than being layered by their mass. The mesosphere is 35 km thick.
Ozone is present in stratosphere and protect us from harmful ultraviolet radiations. Stratosphere is present about 20-25 km above the earth surface.

questions.text: Secondary structure of protein includes:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: An alpha helix is a common type of secondary protein structure. It is a right-handed coiled structure in which a single polypeptide chain twists around itself. This structure is stabilized by hydrogen bonds formed between the carbonyl oxygen of one amino acid residue and the amide hydrogen of an amino acid residue four residues down the chain. Alpha helices are often found in regions of proteins that span cell membranes or in the interior of globular proteins.

Alpha helices (A) and beta sheets (C) are types of secondary protein structures. Alpha helices are right-handed coiled structures, while beta sheets consist of beta strands connected laterally by at least two or three backbone hydrogen bonds, forming a pleated sheet. These structures are part of the secondary structure of proteins, which refers to the local folding patterns of the polypeptide backbone.

Beta sheets (β-sheets) are another type of secondary protein structure. They consist of beta strands, which are segments of the polypeptide chain that run alongside each other. Beta strands are connected laterally by hydrogen bonds between the carbonyl oxygen of one strand and the amide hydrogen of an adjacent strand. Beta sheets can be either parallel (with the strands running in the same direction) or antiparallel (with the strands running in opposite directions). Beta sheets are often found in the core of proteins or as part of protein-protein interaction surfaces.

3-dimensional folding refers to the overall arrangement of the polypeptide chain in space .It encompasses both secondary and tertiary structures. Tertiary structure refers to the specific three-dimensional arrangement of secondary structural elements (such as alpha helices and beta sheets) and other regions of the protein. Tertiary structure is stabilized by various interactions including hydrogen bonds, disulfide bonds, hydrophobic interactions, and electrostatic interactions. The final 3D structure of a protein is crucial for its function, as it determines how the protein interacts with other molecules and performs its biological role.

questions.explanation: Alpha helices (A) and beta sheets (C) are types of secondary protein structures. questions.text: Which of the following elements is not present in all proteins?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Carbon is a fundamental element present in all organic molecules, including proteins. It serves as the backbone of amino acids, the building blocks of proteins, and is crucial for forming the diverse structures and functions exhibited by proteins.

Hydrogen is another essential element found in all proteins. It forms covalent bonds with carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur atoms within amino acids, contributing to the three-dimensional structure and stability of proteins through various types of interactions, such as hydrogen bonding.

Sulfur is not present in all proteins. While some proteins contain amino acids with sulfur atoms in their side chains, such as cysteine and methionine, not all proteins incorporate these sulfur-containing amino acids. Therefore, sulfur is not a universally present element in all proteins.

Nitrogen is a crucial element in proteins, being a component of amino acids, the monomeric units that make up proteins. Nitrogen is found in the amino groups (-NH2) of amino acids, which participate in peptide bond formation during protein synthesis, contributing to the primary structure of proteins.

questions.text: Enzyme used for the treatment of blood cancer in children is:

options.explanation: Cellulose is not an enzyme used for the treatment of blood cancer in children. It is a structural polysaccharide found in plants and is not used therapeutically in this context.

Urease is not used for the treatment of blood cancer in children. It is an enzyme that catalyzes the hydrolysis of urea into ammonia and carbon dioxide and is primarily involved in nitrogen metabolism.

L-asparaginase is the correct option because it is an enzyme used in the treatment of blood cancer in children, particularly acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL). L-asparaginase works by depleting the levels of the amino acid asparagine in the blood, which is necessary for the survival of leukemia cells. questions.text: Rate of enzymatic reaction is directly proportional to the concentration of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: While the concentration of enzyme can influence the rate of reaction, the relationship is not linear. Enzyme concentration can affect the reaction rate up to a certain point of substrate saturation, beyond which increasing enzyme concentration does not significantly increase the rate of reaction.

The rate of an enzymatic reaction is directly proportional to the concentration of substrate. This is because enzymes catalyze reactions by binding to substrates and converting them into products. When there is more substrate available, there are more opportunities for the enzyme to bind to substrate molecules and catalyze the reaction. However, once all enzyme molecules are saturated with substrate, increasing the enzyme concentration will not further increase the reaction rate. Therefore, the rate of enzymatic reaction is directly related to the concentration of substrate.

While both enzyme and substrate concentrations influence the rate of enzymatic reaction, the rate is primarily determined by the concentration of substrate, as described above. Enzyme concentration can affect the rate only up to a certain point of substrate saturation.

The concentration of product does not directly influence the rate of enzymatic reaction. In fact, the accumulation of product can often inhibit the enzyme through feedback inhibition or other regulatory mechanisms. Therefore, the rate of enzymatic reaction is not directly proportional to the concentration of enzyme and product.

The substrate molecule is then able to react with ammonia, water, or carbon dioxide to form a new product. questions.text: Which one of the following organic acids is the most reactive and the strongest acid?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Formic acid is the simplest carboxylic acid. It has a pKa value of 3.75, which is the lowest among the given acids. The pKa value is a measure of the acidity of a molecule. A lower pKa value means that the molecule is more acidic. The other acids have the following pKa values: CH3COOH: pKa = 4.76 CH3CH2COOH: pKa = 5.07 CH3CH2CH2COOH: pKa = 4.89 As you can see, the pKa value of formic acid is lower than the pKa values of the other acids. This means that formic acid is more acidic than the other acids. Formic acid is more reactive than the other acids because it has a smaller size and a more electronegative carbon atom. The smaller size of formic acid makes it more likely to react with other molecules. The more electronegative carbon atom of formic acid makes it more likely to donate a proton to other molecules.
Acetic acid is a weak acid compared to the other options. It partially dissociates in water, releasing H+ ions, but it's not as reactive or strong as some other organic acids.
Propionic acid is also a weak acid, similar to acetic acid. It's not as reactive or strong as stronger organic acids.
Butyric acid is also a weak acid. It's longer in chain length compared to acetic and propionic acid, but still not considered highly reactive or strong in terms of acidity..

questions.text: Carbosylic acids have greater melting and boiling points than alcohols because they have stronger?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Carboxylic acids can form strong hydrogen bonds due to the presence of the carboxyl group (-COOH). This allows molecules of carboxylic acids to attract each other more strongly, resulting in higher melting and boiling points compared to alcohols, which primarily rely on weaker dipole-dipole forces.

Dipole-dipole forces is not as strong as hydrogen bonding. While both carboxylic acids and alcohols can exhibit dipole-dipole interactions due to their polar nature, hydrogen bonding is a specific type of dipole-dipole interaction that is much stronger.

Debye forces refers to the interactions between permanent and induced dipoles in molecules. While these forces exist in both carboxylic acids and alcohols, they are not as significant as hydrogen bonding in carboxylic acids.

Van der Waals forces is a general term encompassing all intermolecular forces, including dipole-dipole interactions and dispersion forces. While carboxylic acids and alcohols both experience Van der Waals forces, the presence of hydrogen bonding in carboxylic acids makes them stronger and thus leads to higher melting and boiling points.

questions.text: Which of the following derivatives of carboxylic acid is least reactive?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Acid halides, also known as acid halides, are highly reactive due to the presence of a highly electronegative halogen atom (such as chlorine, bromine, or iodine) attached to the carbonyl carbon. This makes them susceptible to nucleophilic attack.

Acid amides are the least reactive among the given derivatives of carboxylic acids. This is because amides have a resonance structure that stabilizes the carbonyl group, making them less susceptible to nucleophilic attack compared to other derivatives.

Esters are more reactive than amides but less reactive than acid halides. They undergo nucleophilic acyl substitution reactions, where the carbonyl carbon is attacked by a nucleophile, leading to the substitution of the alkoxy group. However, the presence of the less electronegative oxygen atom in the ester group makes it less reactive compared to acid halides.

Acid anhydrides are more reactive than esters but less reactive than acid halides. They can undergo nucleophilic acyl substitution reactions similar to esters, but their reactivity is enhanced due to the presence of two carbonyl groups in close proximity, facilitating intramolecular reactions.

questions.explanation: Acid amides are the least reactive among the given derivatives of carboxylic acids. This is because amides have a resonance structure that stabilizes the carbonyl group, making them less susceptible to nucleophilic attack compared to other derivatives.

questions.text: Which of the following reagents helps us to distinguish between methanoic acid and ethanoic acid?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A is not the correct answer. Litmus solution is a pH indicator used to determine whether a substance is acidic or basic, but it cannot specifically distinguish between methanoic acid and ethanoic acid.

Option B is the correct answer. Tolien's reagent, also known as Fehling's solution B, is used to distinguish between methanoic acid and ethanoic acid. It is a mixture of copper sulfate (CuSO4) and potassium sodium tartrate (Rochelle salt). Methanoic acid (formic acid) reacts with Tolien's reagent to produce a brick-red precipitate of cuprous oxide (Cu2O). On the other hand, ethanoic acid (acetic acid) does not react with Tolien's reagent.

Option C is not the correct answer. Alcoholic silver nitrate (AgNO3) is used to distinguish between aliphatic and aromatic aldehydes or ketones, but it is not specific to distinguishing between methanoic acid and ethanoic acid.

Option D is not the correct answer. Sodium bicarbonate (baking soda) is used to test the presence of carboxylic acids. Both methanoic acid and ethanoic acid will react with sodium bicarbonate to produce carbon dioxide gas, but this test does not distinguish between the two acids.

questions.explanation: The correct reagent that helps distinguish between methanoic acid and ethanoic acid is B) Tolien's reagent (Fehling's solution B).

questions.text: The sequence of the amino acids combined in a peptide chain is referred to as the_ of protein:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A is the correct answer. The primary structure of a protein refers to the linear sequence of amino acids linked together in a specific order. This sequence is determined by the genetic information encoded in the DNA of the gene that codes for the protein. The primary structure plays a crucial role in defining the overall function and shape of the protein.

Option B is not the correct answer. The secondary structure of a protein refers to the local folding patterns that result from hydrogen bonding between amino acids in the peptide chain. Common secondary structures include alpha helices and beta sheets.

Option C is not the correct answer. The tertiary structure of a protein refers to the three-dimensional folding and arrangement of the entire polypeptide chain. This folding is stabilized by various types of interactions between amino acid side chains.

Option D is not the correct answer. The quaternary structure of a protein refers to the spatial arrangement and interaction between multiple polypeptide chains (subunits) in a multisubunit protein complex. Proteins with quaternary structure have multiple subunits that work together to form the functional protein.

questions.explanation: The correct term used to refer to the sequence of amino acids in a peptide chain that makes up the protein is A) Primary structure.

questions.text: The_ structure of a protein is a regular coiling or zigzagging of polypeptide chains caused by hydrogen bonding between \ NH and C = O groups of amino acids near each other in-the chains.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A is not the correct answer. The primary structure of a protein refers to the linear sequence of amino acids linked together in a specific order. It does not involve coiling or zigzagging of the polypeptide chain.

Option B is the correct answer. The secondary structure of a protein refers to the regular coiling (alpha helix) or zigzagging (beta-sheet) of the polypeptide chain caused by hydrogen bonding between NH and C=O groups of amino acids that are close to each other in the chain. These hydrogen bonds stabilize the local folding patterns.

Option C is not the correct answer. The tertiary structure of a protein refers to the three-dimensional folding and arrangement of the entire polypeptide chain, including the interactions between distant amino acid residues. It is a higher level of organization compared to the secondary structure.

Option D is not the correct answer. The quaternary structure of a protein refers to the spatial arrangement and interactions between multiple polypeptide chains (subunits) in a multisubunit protein complex. It does not involve the coiling or zigzagging of a single polypeptide chain.

questions.explanation: The correct term used to describe the regular coiling or zigzagging of polypeptide chains caused by hydrogen bonding between NH and C=O groups of amino acids near each other in the chains is B) Secondary structure.

Option B is correct. Carbonic anhydrase is an enzyme that contains zinc (Zn) as a cofactor. It catalyzes the conversion of carbon dioxide and water to carbonic acid, an essential reaction in maintaining acid-base balance in the body.

Option C is correct. Glucose-6-phosphatase is an enzyme that contains magnesium (Mg) as a cofactor. It plays a key role in gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis by catalyzing the hydrolysis of glucose-6-phosphate to release free glucose.
Option D is incorrect. The mismatched pair is D. The correct name of the enzyme is glucose-6-phosphatase, not glucosephosphatase. Also, thiamine (vitamin B1) is not a cofactor for this enzyme.

questions.text: Carboxylic acids are formed by the hydrolysis of_?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Carboxylic acids are commonly formed by the hydrolysis of esters. Esters are organic compounds that are derived from carboxylic acids through the reaction known as esterification. In esterification, a carboxylic acid reacts with an alcohol in the presence of an acid catalyst to form an ester and water. The reverse reaction, hydrolysis, involves the breaking of the ester bond by water molecules to reform the carboxylic acid and alcohol. Nitriles, also known as cyanides, are organic compounds containing a cyano group (C≡N). The hydrolysis of nitriles involves the reaction of a nitrile compound with water, resulting in the formation of a carboxylic acid. The hydrolysis of nitriles can occur under both acidic and basic conditions. In acidic hydrolysis, an acid catalyst is used to facilitate the reaction. The nitrile is protonated by the acid catalyst, forming an iminium ion intermediate, which then undergoes hydrolysis to yield a carboxylic acid.

Amines are organic compounds that contain a nitrogen atom bonded to one or more alkyl or aryl groups. They do not hydrolyze to form carboxylic acids. Instead, amines undergo different reactions based on their nature and the reaction conditions. Carboxylic acids can be synthesized from a variety of other reactions, such as the oxidation of primary alcohols, the hydrolysis of esters or amides, and the carbonation of Grignard reagents, among others.

Alkenes and alkynes are unsaturated hydrocarbons that contain carbon-carbon double and triple bonds, respectively. Hydrolysis, which involves the reaction with water, typically does not lead to the formation of carboxylic acids from alkenes or alkynes. Instead, alkenes and alkynes can undergo addition reactions with water, resulting in the formation of alcohols. The reaction is known as hydration and is catalyzed by acids, such as sulfuric acid (H2SO4) or phosphoric acid (H3PO4), or by strong acid catalysts like sulfuric acid absorbed on silica gel (known as sulfuric acid/silica gel). For example, in the hydration of an alkene like ethene (C2H4), the reaction can be represented as follows: C2H4 + H2O → CH3CH2OH In this reaction, ethene reacts with water in the presence of an acid catalyst to yield ethanol (an alcohol), not a carboxylic acid. Similarly, alkynes can undergo hydration to form ketones or aldehydes, depending on the reaction conditions, rather than carboxylic acids.

The hydrolysis of carboxylic acids typically does not yield esters or alcohols. Instead, carboxylic acids undergo hydrolysis to form carboxylate salts or carboxylate anions in the presence of a base or alkali. The hydrolysis of carboxylic acids can occur under both acidic and basic conditions. In acid hydrolysis, the carboxylic acid reacts with water in the presence of an acid catalyst, resulting in the formation of a carboxylate salt and a hydronium ion. For example, the acid hydrolysis of acetic acid (CH3COOH) can be represented as follows: CH3COOH + H2O ⇌ CH3COO- + H3O+ In this reaction, acetic acid reacts with water, and in the presence of an acid catalyst, it forms acetate ion (CH3COO-) and hydronium ion (H3O+). In basic hydrolysis, the carboxylic acid reacts with water in the presence of a base or alkali, resulting in the formation of a carboxylate anion and hydroxide ion. For example, the base hydrolysis of acetic acid can be represented as follows: CH3COOH + OH- ⇌ CH3COO- + H2O In this reaction, acetic acid reacts with hydroxide ion, and it forms acetate ion (CH3COO-) and water. Therefore, the hydrolysis of carboxylic acids leads to the formation of carboxylate salts or carboxylate anions, depending on the reaction conditions, rather than the formation of esters or alcohols.

Here are a few common ways in which CO can be formed: Incomplete Combustion: When a carbon-containing fuel is burned with limited oxygen supply, such as in a poorly ventilated or incomplete combustion environment, carbon monoxide can be produced. For example, incomplete combustion of fossil fuels like coal, gasoline, or natural gas can generate CO. Industrial Processes: CO can be formed as a byproduct in various industrial processes. For instance, during the production of iron and steel, carbon monoxide is often produced as a reducing agent in the blast furnace. Additionally, certain chemical reactions and industrial processes involving carbon-containing compounds can yield CO as a side product. Automobile Emissions: In internal combustion engines, especially those with incomplete combustion or malfunctioning exhaust systems, the burning of fuel can produce carbon monoxide. This is a significant contributor to CO emissions from vehicles. Natural Processes: CO can also be generated through natural processes. For instance, volcanic activity and wildfires release carbon monoxide into the atmosphere.

The presence of a catalyst, such as sulfuric acid, helps to drive the reaction forward and increase the yield of the desired ester product.

Here are the two methods for the formation of propanol: Oxidation of Propanal: Propanal (also known as propionaldehyde) can be oxidized to form propanol by using a suitable oxidizing agent. One common oxidizing agent used for this conversion is a mild solution of chromic acid (CrO3) or potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) in the presence of sulfuric acid (H2SO4). The balanced chemical equation for the oxidation of propanal to propanol is as follows: CH3CH2CHO + [O] → CH3CH2CH2OH In this reaction, propanal is oxidized by the oxidizing agent, resulting in the formation of propanol. Reduction of Propanone: Propanone, also known as acetone, can be reduced to form propanol through a reduction reaction. The reduction is typically carried out using reducing agents such as sodium borohydride (NaBH4) or lithium aluminum hydride (LiAlH4). The balanced chemical equation for the reduction of propanone to propanol is as follows: CH3COCH3 + 2H2 → CH3CH2CH2OH In this reaction, propanone is reduced by the reducing agent, resulting in the formation of propanol. Both methods allow the formation of propanol from different starting materials. The choice of method depends on the availability of the starting compound and the desired reaction conditions.

Here's the balanced chemical equation for the formation of ethanal from ethanol (CH3CH2OH): CH3CH2OH + [O] -> CH3CHO + H2O In this reaction, ethanol is oxidized by the oxidizing agent, and it results in the formation of ethanal (acetaldehyde) and water.

No, CH3CH2OH, which represents ethanol, does not directly evolve carbon dioxide with sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3). Ethanol (CH3CH2OH) is a primary alcohol and does not readily react with sodium bicarbonate to produce carbon dioxide. Sodium bicarbonate typically reacts with acids to release carbon dioxide gas.

CH3CH2COOH, which represents propanoic acid, can evolve carbon dioxide with sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3). Propanoic acid is a carboxylic acid and can react with sodium bicarbonate to produce carbon dioxide gas. The reaction can be represented as follows: CH3CH2COOH + NaHCO3 -> CH3CH2COONa + H2O + CO2 In this reaction, carbon dioxide gas is evolved along with water and the salt sodium propanoate (CH3CH2COONa). The carbon dioxide gas bubbles out, resulting in visible effervescence. So, when propanoic acid (CH3CH2COOH) reacts with sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), it does indeed produce carbon dioxide gas.

CH3COOOCH3 does not evolve carbon dioxide with sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3). CH3COOOCH3 represents methyl ethanoate, which is an ester. Esters typically do not react with sodium bicarbonate to produce carbon dioxide. Sodium bicarbonate primarily reacts with acids to release carbon dioxide gas. In this case, if you are looking to produce carbon dioxide gas using sodium bicarbonate, you would need an acidic compound rather than an ester. Esters are generally unreactive with sodium bicarbonate under normal conditions. Therefore, CH3COOOCH3 (methyl ethanoate) does not evolve carbon dioxide with sodium bicarbonate.

options.explanation: Ethanoic acid, also known as acetic acid (CH3COOH), is an example of an aliphatic carboxylic acid rather than an aromatic carboxylic acid. Acetic acid is a two-carbon carboxylic acid with the chemical formula CH3COOH. It belongs to the class of aliphatic carboxylic acids as it does not contain a benzene ring or possess aromatic characteristics. Acetic acid is widely known and used in various applications. It is a key component of vinegar, giving it its characteristic sour taste and pungent smell. Acetic acid is also used in the production of various chemicals, solvents, and plastics. In summary, ethanoic acid (acetic acid) is an example of an aliphatic carboxylic acid rather than an aromatic carboxylic acid.

Butanoic acid (C4H8O2) is an example of an aliphatic carboxylic acid rather than an aromatic carboxylic acid. Butanoic acid, also known as butyric acid, is a four-carbon carboxylic acid with the chemical formula CH3CH2CH2COOH. It belongs to the class of aliphatic carboxylic acids as it does not contain a benzene ring or possess aromatic characteristics. Butanoic acid is known for its strong, unpleasant odor and is often found in rancid butter and certain cheeses. It is also used in various industrial applications, including as a flavoring agent and as a precursor for the production of esters. In summary, butanoic acid is an example of an aliphatic carboxylic acid rather than an aromatic carboxylic acid.

Adipic acid (C6H10O4) is not an example of an aromatic carboxylic acid. It is a saturated dicarboxylic acid with two carboxyl groups (-COOH) attached to a linear aliphatic chain. Adipic acid is primarily used in the production of nylon and other polymeric materials. While adipic acid has six carbon atoms in its structure, it is not considered aromatic because it does not contain a benzene ring or possess aromatic characteristics. Therefore, adipic acid is an example of an aliphatic carboxylic acid rather than an aromatic carboxylic acid.

phthalic acid (C6H4(CO2H)2) is an example of an aromatic carboxylic acid. Phthalic acid contains two carboxyl groups (-CO2H) attached to a benzene ring, making it an aromatic carboxylic acid.
